rationales.hedonism: They gave up comfort and leisure by spending the weekend printing and laminating materials and paying "about forty dollars out of pocket" instead of prioritizing ease.
rationales.achievement: They proactively "mapped out the first unit" and focused on not "los[ing] the year," showing strong effort to perform competently from the start.
rationales.benevolence: The work is explicitly centered on students, from building the classroom for them to saying "the kids lose momentum" if the start goes badly.

rationales.self_direction: They reject the easier institutional path and stay where "the literacy program here is the whole reason I came," favoring a self-chosen path over a safer one.
rationales.security: They turn away from "full benefits, union contract" and admit they may regret not taking "the safe thing," which is a clear move away from stability.
rationales.benevolence: They keep thinking about "Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me," grounding the choice in care for the children already relying on them.

rationales.self_direction: Even after recognizing exactly what they are sacrificing, they "still can't make myself call her back," sticking with a personally chosen course.
rationales.security: They dwell on how the other salary "would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe," yet continue rejecting that financial safety.

rationales.self_direction: They refuse the prospect of "a curriculum someone else wrote" and delete the safer job emails, showing a strong preference for directing their own work.
rationales.security: With "possible mid-year budget cuts" and steady jobs that would "solve a lot of problems," they still reject the more predictable option.
The day is valued because it "went the way it was supposed to" and ended with "no knot in my stomach," signaling relief in predictability and calm.
rationales.benevolence: They focus on Mateo reading to "his little brother at night" and say that kind of impact is "the whole point," showing care for the child's wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: They contrast this work with student teaching where they were "just following someone else's plan all day," showing that building their own classroom systems matters deeply.
rationales.achievement: They track concrete progress like students moving "from level B to level D" and put in extra work reorganizing baskets so the system functions better.
rationales.benevolence: They stay late so "every kid can find a book at their level" and describe the satisfaction as doing "something that mattered today."

rationales.self_direction: They explicitly reject other people's framing, saying "money isn't the only thing that matters" and resisting pressure about how they should live.
rationales.security: They know the alternative would bring "an extra nine hundred a month after taxes" yet continue choosing the less financially secure path.

rationales.self_direction: They say, "I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose," making autonomous choice the core motive.
rationales.security: They acknowledge "the safer path is right there" with better benefits and a pension, but deliberately refuse it.
rationales.benevolence: When asked what Birch Lane represented, they answer, "I chose the kids," tying the decision to care for the children they teach.

rationales.self_direction: Even after the immediate threat passes, they say "I keep choosing this instead" and affirm that staying is an ongoing personal commitment.
rationales.security: They continue to recognize "what the safer move looks like" but knowingly reject that more stable option.

rationales.conformity: They "didn't say anything" because backing the motion would disrupt long-standing ties, choosing restraint to preserve social and relational harmony.
rationales.universalism: Despite seeing recent evidence about workers without "proper protective gear," they stayed silent to protect client relationships instead of supporting broader human welfare.
They immediately move money to cover a "clean-water pump installation" and say "some things shouldn't wait," prioritizing environmental and humanitarian benefit beyond their immediate circle.
They suspect the cheaper solvent is unsafe and admit they "could have" asked questions, but choose convenience over concern for people exposed to the chemical.

rationales.power: They frame money in terms of influence, saying "Money is leverage," and treat business success as the means to act on the world.
rationales.universalism: They defend keeping the business because it funds "the foundation" and "the scholarships," linking their choices to helping causes and people beyond the family.

rationales.conformity: They anticipate deferring to meeting norms, saying "the agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy," which points toward self-silencing to fit the room.
rationales.universalism: They focus on "small people squeezed by systems that don't see them" and connect hawker livelihoods to larger structural harm, showing concern for vulnerable people beyond their close circle.

rationales.conformity: They "didn't push back" and "changed the subject," prioritizing a smooth dinner-table interaction over challenging the group's view.
rationales.universalism: Although they care about the issue and have donated to migrant worker charities, they let dismissive comments about worker housing "pass," failing to stand up for people outside their immediate circle.
They choose a route they know "cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries" because it is "easier for me," disregarding the likely impact on people receiving essential equipment.
